lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Wilt thou be gone? it is not yet near day:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: It was the nightingale, and not the lark,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That pierced the fearful hollow of thine ear;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Nightly she sings on yon pomegranate-tree:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Believe me, love, it was the nightingale.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: It was the lark, the herald of the morn,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: No nightingale: look, love, what envious streaks

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Do lace the severing clouds in yonder east:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Night’s candles are burnt out, and jocund day

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Stands tiptoe on the misty mountain tops.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I must be gone and live, or stay and die.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Yon light is not day-light, I know it, I:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: It is some meteor that the sun exhales,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To be to thee this night a torch-bearer,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And light thee on thy way to Mantua:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Therefore stay yet; thou need’st not to be gone.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Let me be ta’en, let me be put to death;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I am content, so thou wilt have it so.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I’ll say yon grey is not the morning’s eye,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: ’Tis but the pale reflex of Cynthia’s brow;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Nor that is not the lark, whose notes do beat

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The vaulty heaven so high above our heads:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I have more care to stay than will to go:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Come, death, and welcome! Juliet wills it so.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: How is’t, my soul? let’s talk; it is not day.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: It is, it is: hie hence, be gone, away!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: It is the lark that sings so out of tune,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Straining harsh discords and unpleasing sharps.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Some say the lark makes sweet division;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: This doth not so, for she divideth us:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Some say the lark and loathed toad change eyes;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, now I would they had changed voices too!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Since arm from arm that voice doth us affray,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Hunting thee hence with hunt’s-up to the day.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, now be gone; more light and light it grows.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: More light and light; more dark and dark our woes!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Madam!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Nurse?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Your lady mother is coming to your chamber:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: The day is broke; be wary, look about.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Then, window, let day in, and let life out.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Farewell, farewell! one kiss, and I’ll descend. He goeth down.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Art thou gone so? love, lord, ay, husband, friend!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I must hear from thee every day in the hour,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For in a minute there are many days:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, by this count I shall be much in years

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Ere I again behold my Romeo!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Farewell!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I will omit no opportunity

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That may convey my greetings, love, to thee.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O think’st thou we shall ever meet again?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I doubt it not; and all these woes shall serve

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: For sweet discourses in our time to come.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O God, I have an ill-divining soul!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Methinks I see thee, now thou art below,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: As one dead in the bottom of a tomb:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Either my eyesight fails, or thou look’st pale.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And trust me, love, in my eye so do you:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Dry sorrow drinks our blood. Adieu, adieu!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O fortune, fortune! all men call thee fickle:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If thou art fickle, what dost thou with him

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That is renown’d for faith? Be fickle, fortune;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For then, I hope, thou wilt not keep him long,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But send him back.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Ho, daughter! are you up?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Who is’t that calls? is it my lady mother?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Is she not down so late, or up so early?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What unaccustom’d cause procures her hither?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Why, how now, Juliet!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Madam, I am not well.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Evermore weeping for your cousin’s death?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: What, wilt thou wash him from his grave with tears?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: An if thou couldst, thou couldst not make him live;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Therefore, have done: some grief shows much of love;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: But much of grief shows still some want of wit.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Yet let me weep for such a feeling loss.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: So shall you feel the loss, but not the friend

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Which you weep for.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Feeling so the loss,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I cannot choose but ever weep the friend.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Well, girl, thou weep’st not so much for his death,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: As that the villain lives which slaughter’d him.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What villain, madam?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: That same villain, Romeo.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Villain and he be many miles asunder.—

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: God Pardon him! I do, with all my heart;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And yet no man like he doth grieve my heart.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: That is, because the traitor murderer lives.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Ay, madam, from the reach of these my hands:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Would none but I might venge my cousin’s death!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: We will have vengeance for it, fear thou not:

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Then weep no more. I’ll send to one in Mantua,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Where that same banish’d runagate doth live,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Shall give him such an unaccustom’d dram,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: That he shall soon keep Tybalt company:

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: And then, I hope, thou wilt be satisfied.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Indeed, I never shall be satisfied

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: With Romeo, till I behold him—dead—

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Is my poor heart so for a kinsman vex’d:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Madam, if you could find out but a man

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To bear a poison, I would temper it;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That Romeo should, upon receipt thereof,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Soon sleep in quiet. O, how my heart abhors

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To hear him named, and cannot come to him,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To wreak the love I bore my cousin

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Upon his body that hath slaughter’d him!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Find thou the means, and I’ll find such a man.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: But now I’ll tell thee joyful tidings, girl.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And joy comes well in such a needy time:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What are they, I beseech your ladyship?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Well, well, thou hast a careful father, child;

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: One who, to put thee from thy heaviness,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Hath sorted out a sudden day of joy,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: That thou expect’st not nor I look’d not for.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Madam, in happy time, what day is that?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Marry, my child, early next Thursday morn,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: The gallant, young and noble gentleman,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: The County Paris, at Saint Peter’s Church,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Shall happily make thee there a joyful bride.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Now, by Saint Peter’s Church and Peter too,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: He shall not make me there a joyful bride.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I wonder at this haste; that I must wed

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Ere he, that should be husband, comes to woo.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I pray you, tell my lord and father, madam,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I will not marry yet; and, when I do, I swear,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: It shall be Romeo, whom you know I hate,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Rather than Paris. These are news indeed!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Here comes your father; tell him so yourself,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: And see how he will take it at your hands.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: When the sun sets, the air doth drizzle dew;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: But for the sunset of my brother’s son

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: It rains downright.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: How now! a conduit, girl? what, still in tears?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Evermore showering? In one little body

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Thou counterfeit’st a bark, a sea, a wind;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: For still thy eyes, which I may call the sea,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Do ebb and flow with tears; the bark thy body is,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Sailing in this salt flood; the winds, thy sighs;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Who, raging with thy tears, and they with them,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Without a sudden calm, will overset

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Thy tempest-tossed body. How now, wife!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Have you deliver’d to her our decree?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Ay, sir; but she will none, she gives you thanks.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: I would the fool were married to her grave!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Soft! take me with you, take me with you, wife.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: How! will she none? doth she not give us thanks?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Is she not proud? doth she not count her blest,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Unworthy as she is, that we have wrought

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: So worthy a gentleman to be her bridegroom?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Not proud, you have; but thankful, that you have:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Proud can I never be of what I hate;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: But thankful even for hate, that is meant love.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: How now, how now, chop-logic! What is this?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: “Proud,” and “I thank you,” and “I thank you not;”

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And yet “not proud:” mistress minion, you,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Thank me no thankings, nor proud me no prouds,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: But fettle your fine joints ’gainst Thursday next,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: To go with Paris to Saint Peter’s Church,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Or I will drag thee on a hurdle thither.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Out, you green-sickness carrion! out, you baggage!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: You tallow-face!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Fie, fie! what, are you mad?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Good father, I beseech you on my knees,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Hear me with patience but to speak a word.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Hang thee, young baggage! disobedient wretch!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: I tell thee what: get thee to church o’ Thursday,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Or never after look me in the face:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Speak not, reply not, do not answer me;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: My fingers itch. Wife, we scarce thought us blest

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: That God had lent us but this only child;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: But now I see this one is one too much,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And that we have a curse in having her:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Out on her, hilding!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: God in heaven bless her!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: You are to blame, my lord, to rate her so.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And why, my lady wisdom? hold your tongue,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Good prudence; smatter with your gossips, go.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I speak no treason.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: O, God ye god-den.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: May not one speak?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Peace, you mumbling fool!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Utter your gravity o’er a gossip’s bowl;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: For here we need it not.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: You are too hot.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: God’s bread! it makes me mad:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Day, night, hour, tide, time, work, play,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Alone, in company, still my care hath been

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: To have her match’d: and having now provided

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: A gentleman of noble parentage,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Of fair demesnes, youthful, and nobly train’d,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Stuff’d, as they say, with honourable parts,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Proportion’d as one’s thought would wish a man;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And then to have a wretched puling fool,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: A whining mammet, in her fortune’s tender,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: To answer “I’ll not wed; I cannot love,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: I am too young; I pray you, pardon me.”

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: But, an you will not wed, I’ll pardon you:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Graze where you will, you shall not house with me:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Look to’t, think on’t, I do not use to jest.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Thursday is near; lay hand on heart, advise:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: An you be mine, I’ll give you to my friend;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: An you be not, hang, beg, starve, die in the streets,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: For, by my soul, I’ll ne’er acknowledge thee,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Nor what is mine shall never do thee good:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Trust to’t, bethink you; I’ll not be forsworn.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Is there no pity sitting in the clouds,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That sees into the bottom of my grief?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, sweet my mother, cast me not away!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Delay this marriage for a month, a week;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or, if you do not, make the bridal bed

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: In that dim monument where Tybalt lies.

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Talk not to me, for I’ll not speak a word:

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Do as thou wilt, for I have done with thee.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O God!—O nurse, how shall this be prevented?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: My husband is on earth, my faith in heaven;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: How shall that faith return again to earth,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Unless that husband send it me from heaven

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: By leaving earth? comfort me, counsel me.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Alack, alack, that heaven should practise stratagems

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Upon so soft a subject as myself!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What say’st thou? hast thou not a word of joy?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Some comfort, nurse.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Faith, here it is.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Romeo is banish’d; and all the world to nothing,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: That he dares ne’er come back to challenge you;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Or, if he do, it needs must be by stealth.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Then, since the case so stands as now it doth,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I think it best you married with the county.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O, he’s a lovely gentleman!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Romeo’s a dishclout to him: an eagle, madam,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Hath not so green, so quick, so fair an eye

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: As Paris hath. Beshrew my very heart,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I think you are happy in this second match,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: For it excels your first; or if it did not,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Your first is dead; or ’twere as good he were,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: As living here and you no use of him.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Speakest thou from thy heart?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And from my soul too;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Or else beshrew them both.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Amen!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: What?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Well, thou hast comforted me marvellous much.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Go in; and tell my lady I am gone,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Having displeased my father, to Laurence’ cell,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To make confession and to be absolved.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Marry, I will; and this is wisely done.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Ancient damnation! O most wicked fiend!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Is it more sin to wish me thus forsworn,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or to dispraise my lord with that same tongue

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Which she hath praised him with above compare

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: So many thousand times? Go, counsellor;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Thou and my bosom henceforth shall be twain.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I’ll to the friar, to know his remedy:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If all else fail, myself have power to die.

stage_directions.text: Enter Romeo and Juliet above, at the window.

glosses.definition: Juliet's opening line of the dawn-parting — the play's aubade
the bird of night — lovers' bird
frightened; anxious
bird of dawn — the morning's announcer
the fruit tree in Capulet's orchard
cheerful day
the dawn light is a meteor, not dawn itself — Juliet's desperate astronomy
the moon (Cynthia = Diana, goddess of the moon) — Romeo yields to Juliet by naming dawn as moonlight

glosses.phrase: more care to stay than will to go
glosses.definition: Romeo's willingness to die for her
high, shrill notes
a rapid run of musical notes
this lark, however, does not separate us kindly
chasing you from me
frighten away
the toad swallowed the lark's eye in folk belief — an ugly exchange
morning horn-call announcing the hunt — and, by extension, dawn itself

glosses.phrase: More light and light it grows
glosses.definition: the dawn wins the argument

glosses.phrase: More light and light; more dark and dark our woes!
glosses.definition: brighter day = darker sorrow; the pivot of the scene
Juliet, addressed formally
by descending the rope ladder
a soul with a gift for foreseeing ill — Juliet's prophetic dread

glosses.phrase: As one dead in the bottom of a tomb
glosses.definition: Juliet imagines Romeo entombed — the vision of 5.3
dry sorrow = grief without tears; popular belief that grief literally drained the blood
fickle fortune — its inconstancy
grief that can be felt; real bereavement
peasant; rogue — Lady Capulet's word for Romeo
Lady Capulet bitterly vows revenge on Romeo
vagabond; fugitive
a dose of poison such as he's not used to

glosses.phrase: thou expect’st not nor I look’d not for
glosses.definition: the surprise announcement of the Paris wedding
Capulet's chosen date for Juliet's wedding to Paris
too early

glosses.phrase: Not proud, you have; but thankful, that you have
glosses.definition: Juliet's threadbare courtesy — she declines Paris but thanks them
hussy; spoiled one — Capulet's bitter word for his daughter
prepare yourself; get ready
the sled on which traitors were dragged to execution
pale, chlorotic flesh — Capulet's vicious insult
loose woman; worthless girl
good-for-nothing wretch
hair-splitting reasoner
rebellious child
by the Eucharist — Capulet's oath
estates
qualities
beg for bread
family tomb — the one Juliet will soon enter
sarcasm — the Nurse turned advisor
curse — the Nurse's mild oath
Paris's gaze, quick and sharp
dishcloth — the Nurse's dismissal of Romeo
Juliet's dawning recognition of the Nurse's betrayal
Juliet bitterly agrees — but to a different prayer
aged devil — Juliet's verdict on the Nurse who once nursed her

glosses.phrase: Thou and my bosom henceforth shall be twain
glosses.definition: you and my heart are now severed — Juliet's final break with her nurse

glosses.phrase: If all else fail, myself have power to die
glosses.definition: Juliet's turn to self-agency — the potion or the dagger

annotations.title: The aubade — a dawn-song sung in the wrong direction
annotations.body: The aubade is the lyric tradition of lovers parting at dawn — typically she wishes the night would continue; he acknowledges that it must not. Shakespeare runs the convention with a twist. Juliet argues that the bird they hear is the nightingale (night's bird) rather than the lark (dawn's); Romeo at first concedes ('Let me be ta'en, let me be put to death'), accepting the fiction for the sake of one more hour. Then Juliet relents: 'It is the lark that sings so out of tune.' The reversal is devastating — once Juliet admits the dawn, it is real, and Romeo must go. The aubade tradition is courtly and erotic; here it is saturated with dread ('More light and light; more dark and dark our woes!'). Juliet's prophetic vision of Romeo 'As one dead in the bottom of a tomb' extends the tragedy's inexorable forward motion.
sources: Jonathan Saville, The Medieval Erotic Alba (1972)
Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

annotations.title: Capulet's rage — the patriarch's extreme response
annotations.body: When Juliet refuses to marry Paris, Capulet's response is terrifying: 'Out, you green-sickness carrion! out, you baggage! / You tallow-face!' He threatens to drag her to the church 'on a hurdle' (the sled used for traitors' executions), disown her, starve her: 'Hang, beg, starve, die in the streets!' The violence is startling partly because Capulet has been genial elsewhere (1.2, 1.5). Shakespeare shows a father whose dynastic investment in his daughter (see 1.2's 'hopeful lady of my earth') curdles into rage the moment she asserts will. Lady Capulet's 'I would the fool were married to her grave' compounds the horror. This is not a study of uniquely bad parents but of the Elizabethan household under pressure: a patriarchal house discovering that its dynasty depends on the consent of a child.
sources: Lawrence Stone, The Family, Sex and Marriage in England 1500–1800 (1977)

annotations.title: The Nurse's betrayal — 'ancient damnation'
annotations.body: Facing the catastrophe her parents have delivered, Juliet turns to the Nurse who has loved her since the breast. The Nurse's counsel is pragmatic: marry Paris ('He's a lovely gentleman! / Romeo's a dishclout to him'). For Juliet this is betrayal absolute. Her aside — 'Ancient damnation! O most wicked fiend!' and 'Thou and my bosom henceforth shall be twain' — is the play's most painful break in adolescent relationship. She does not show the Nurse her shock; she protects the pretense and reserves her judgment for soliloquy. This is the moment Juliet is fully alone. From here, she will decide things on her own — the Friar becomes her only adviser, and her final decisions (the potion, the dagger) are her own. The Nurse does not reappear substantively after this scene.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

trials.prompt: The opening of 3.5 is a dawn-scene in a traditional lyric form. What is it?
options: An elegy
An aubade — the medieval/Renaissance dawn-parting lyric of lovers
A pastoral dialogue
A prothalamion

trials.prompt: Why does Juliet initially insist the bird they hear is the nightingale, not the lark?
options: She cannot tell birds apart
The nightingale sings by night; she is asking for more night — to keep Romeo from having to leave
She thinks the lark is sick
The lark cannot sing in the orchard

trials.prompt: Capulet's rage against Juliet ('Hang, beg, starve, die in the streets!') reveals —
options: That he has never loved her
A patriarchal dynasty's crisis when its only heir refuses to consent — his affection (1.2) turns to fury when his line depends on her will
That he is drunk
That he does not know who Juliet is

trials.prompt: Juliet's response to the Nurse's advice (marry Paris, forget Romeo) is —
options: Relieved agreement
A total break — 'Ancient damnation!' 'Thou and my bosom henceforth shall be twain' — Juliet is now alone in her decisions
Anger she shows openly to the Nurse
Indifference

trials.prompt: 'More light and light; more dark and dark our woes!' — what rhetorical figure organises this line?
options: Simile
Parallel structure with antithesis (the repetition 'more light and light' mirrored by 'more dark and dark')
Metonymy
Hendiadys
